# Choice of Populations for Cancer Prevention Trials

DOI link for Choice of Populations for Cancer Prevention Trials

Choice of Populations for Cancer Prevention Trials

By Meir J. Stampfer , Walter C. Willett , Charles H. Hennekens

Book [Nutrition and Cancer Prevention](https://www.taylorfrancis.com/books/mono/10.1201/9781003210061/nutrition-cancer-prevention?refId=11d7a60f-f0e8-48fc-b603-f6daaeb2a655&context=ubx)

Click here to navigate to parent product.

Edition 1st Edition

First Published 1988

Imprint CRC Press

Pages 10

Share

## ABSTRACT

The population selected for a cancer prevention trial should be chosen to maximize the feasibility of recruitment and followup, the probability of compliance with all requirements of the trial, and the potential to provide meaningful results which are generalizable to a larger reference population. Primary prevention trials for cancer generally require large numbers of subjects. Selection of compliant participants by means of a run-in period will often prove useful, but the population selected initially should be intrinsically motivated to stay with the trial. The knowledge and interest of physicians probably made them easier to motivate than many other groups. The trial organizers should exert all possible efforts to maintain high compliance in all groups. In the Physicians’ Health Study, the treatment regimen was planned so that subjects take just one pill per day. The pills are packaged in monthly calendar packs with each bubble numbered to correspond to the day of the month.
